t: In adulthood, tics significantly decrease in ~50%, persist in ~30%, and severe form remains in ~20% (Bloch M.H., Leckman J.F. J Psychosom Res 2009).
ADHD 50–60%, OCD 30–50%, anxiety 30%, depression 20%, learning disorder 20–30%, angry outbursts, sleep disorders.
0.77 (Mataix-Cols D. et al. JAMA Psychiatry 2015 family and twin studies, n>200 000 Sweden) — among the most heritable psychiatric disorders.
GWAS (Yu D. et al. Am J Psychiatry 2019) — risk loci identified; no single gene.
SLITRK1, HDC (histidine decarboxylase), CELSR3 — in a small number of families.
High density of OCD and ADHD in family history — genetic overlap.

Reduction in caudate volume, sensorimotor cortical thinning (Sowell E.R. et al. Nat Neurosci 2008).
Preterm birth, perinatal hypoxia — moderately increases risk.
Psychosocial stress — is not a cause for the onset of tics, but influences severity.

(Pediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated with Streptococcal infections / Pediatric Acute-onset Neuropsychiatric Syndrome) — rare; most cases are actually typical Tourette/OCD (see expanded §10).
Eye blinking (most frequent onset), nose sniffing, head shaking, shoulder shrugging, lip play.
Sequential movements — hopping, touching, pirouette; rarely obscene gesture (copropraxia, ~5%).
coughing, sniffling, wheezing, throat clearing, shouting.
word, echolalia (repetition of others' words), palilalia (repetition of own words).
the condition is present (Freeman R.D. et al. Dev Med Child Neurol 2009 international Tourette database, n=3500), and differs from the portrayal in mass media.

(somatic urge, tension) is noted; the tic temporarily relieves this sensation. Tics can be voluntarily suppressed for a short period (seconds to minutes), but this suppression creates internal tension and discomfort — the subsequent tic episode is usually more intense.
— Violence period varies; one tick type replaced by another.
Triggers — stress (positive or negative), excitement, fatigue, specific contexts (e.g., seizure at school).
Tics decrease during sleep, but do not completely disappear.
Peak severity at ages 10–12; decreases in most patients during adolescence.
6.1 Unified diagnostic criteria (DSM-5-TR · ICD-11 · AAN 2019 consensus points)
one or several vocal tics (occurring at different times, not required to be present simultaneously)
Exceptions — not fully explained by substance (e.g., cocaine) or medical condition (Huntington's, postviral encephalitis).
Persistent motor or vocal tic disorder:
Same criteria as Tourette, but duration

Three separate categories (307.21, 307.22, 307.23). "Functional impairment" and "severity" were not included in the diagnostic requirement — merely meeting the criteria is diagnostically sufficient; however, the intervention decision is based on functional impairment.
"Primary tics or tic disorders" — sub-specifications: Tourette, persistent, provisional.

Диагноз является клиническим, записи служат вспомогательным средством; ШГТС (Шкала общей тяжести тиков Йельского университета) для исходных показателей и наблюдения; сопутствующие состояния (СДВГ, ОКР) должны быть обязательно оценены.
In the European context — same criteria; accepts CBIT as first-line non-pharmacological intervention.
Parent and patient interview — onset age, course, triggers, premonitory feeling.
Video recordings — episodes captured at home by family (in clinic context tics often diminish)
(Yale Global Tic Severity Scale, baseline measures and monitoring).
Comorbidity screening — ADHD (Conners, Vanderbilt), OCD (CY-BOCS), anxiety, depression, learning disorder, sleep.
Neurological examination — focal signs, exclusion of choreoathetosis.
Specific clinical suspicion — Huntington's disease (HTT gene), Wilson's disease (ceruloplasmin, copper), neuroacanthocytosis.
PANDAS/PANS suspicion — only if classic clinical picture (rapid onset, OCD + tics + emotional lability post-streptococcal); routine antistreptolysin O tests are not recommended (NIMH 2017).
Rhythmic, longer-lasting (seconds-minutes), no premonitory urge; onset <3 years.
Brief, sudden, involuntary, no premonitory sensation; neurological etiology.
Persistent involuntary movements; cannot be suppressed; neurological examination.
Ego-dystonic purposeful sequences; response to obsession. Comorbid is common.
Rapid onset in adulthood, complex bizarre patterns, copying from mass media; increase observed during COVID-19 (Pringsheim T. et al. Mov Disord Clin Pract 2021)
Rapid onset post-streptococcal; OCD + tics co-occurrence outbreak; rare.
Hepatic and neurological symptoms; Kayser-Fleischer rings, decreased ceruloplasmin.
Typical onset in adulthood; chorea, cognitive decline, family history.

(Yale Global Tic Severity Scale, Leckman J.F. et al. 1989) — separate for motor and vocal tics; frequency, intensity, complexity, interference, number on a 0–5 scale; global impairment 0–50. Baseline measures and treatment monitoring.
No specific laboratory indication for routine tics. Based on clinical suspicion:
Ceruloplasmin, 24-hour urine copper — suspicion of Wilson's disease (especially hepatic or movement adjunct).
Streptococcal antibodies (ASO, anti-DNase B) - only if classic PANDAS clinical picture present; not recommended routinely.
